items.text: When the spirits are low, when the day appears dark, when work becomes monotonous, when hopes seem hardly worth having, just mount a bicycle and go for a good spin down the road, without thought of anything but the ride you are taking.
items.context: Conan Doyle was writing about the health benefits of bicycling, describing his personal practices as a physician and writer during the Victorian cycling craze.
items.publicReaction: The statement was reprinted multiple times, including in Cycling magazine (October 1895) and Scientific American (January 1896), showing it resonated with readers. It remains one of his most quoted passages on wellness.
items.source: Demorest's Family Magazine, Vol. 31, No. 7, article titled 'What Are the Benefits of Bicycling? Testimony of an Enthusiast: Dr. A. Conan Doyle'

items.text: I should dearly love that the world should be ever so little better for my presence...something might be done by throwing all one's weight on the scale of breadth, tolerance, charity, temperance, peace, and kindliness to man and beast.
items.context: Conan Doyle expressed his hopes for his life's impact, reflecting on how he might improve the world through virtuous living and moral influence.
items.publicReaction: This statement from his semi-autobiographical novel became widely quoted as expressing his philosophical ideals and social values.
items.source: The Stark Munro Letters, published by Longmans, Green & Co., London

items.text: I could no more doubt the existence of the phenomena than I could doubt the existence of lions in Africa, though I have been to that continent and have never chanced to see one.
items.context: Conan Doyle wrote to the spiritualist magazine Light to announce his conversion to spiritualism after conducting séances and investigating psychic phenomena.
items.publicReaction: His public declaration of belief in spiritualism surprised many who knew him primarily as a rationalist physician and creator of Sherlock Holmes. It marked the beginning of his lifelong advocacy for the spiritualist cause.
items.source: Letter published in Light, the journal of the London Spiritualistic Alliance

items.text: The subject of psychical research is one upon which I have thought more, and been slower to form my opinion, than upon any other subject whatever.
items.context: Opening statement from his lecture before the London Spiritualist Alliance, where he presented his studied position on spiritualism to a formal audience.
items.publicReaction: The lecture was widely reported in spiritualist and mainstream media. His declaration of serious study lent credibility to the spiritualist movement in certain circles.
items.source: Lecture delivered at the London Spiritualist Alliance, Salon of the Royal Society of British Artists, Suffolk Street, Pall Mall East; published in Light magazine on November 10, 17, and 24, 1917

items.text: Christianity must change or must perish. That is the law of life—that things must adapt themselves or perish.
items.context: Conan Doyle argued that Christianity needed to incorporate spiritualist beliefs to remain relevant to modern believers.
items.publicReaction: The statement provoked debate among religious leaders and conservative critics who viewed spiritualism as incompatible with traditional Christianity.
items.source: The New Revelation, published as pamphlet in Light (1917) and as book by Hodder & Stoughton (1918)

items.text: I do not think that any unprejudiced man can read the facts without acknowledging that the British Government has done its best to avoid war, and the British Army to wage it with humanity.
items.context: Defense of British conduct during the Boer War, written as propaganda to counter international criticism of British military actions.
items.publicReaction: The pamphlet sold approximately 300,000 copies in Britain and was translated into multiple languages, successfully shifting public opinion both domestically and internationally. It led directly to his knighthood.
items.source: The War in South Africa: Its Cause and Conduct, published by George Newnes Ltd.

items.text: For some reason, which may be either arrogance or apathy, the British are very slow to state their case to the world...In view of the persistent slanders to which our politicians and our soldiers have been equally exposed, it becomes a duty which we owe to our national honour to lay the facts before the world.
items.context: Explanation of why he felt compelled to write a defense of British policy during the Boer War for international audiences.
items.publicReaction: His argument resonated with British patriots and earned him the admiration of political leaders, resulting in his knighthood in October 1902.
items.source: The War in South Africa: Its Cause and Conduct

items.text: There is nothing scientifically impossible, so far as I can see, in some people seeing things that are invisible to others.
items.context: Conan Doyle defending the Cottingley Fairies photographs as evidence for the existence of fairies, arguing against scientific dismissal of the evidence.
items.publicReaction: The statement was controversial, with critics mocking Conan Doyle for his credulity. His defense of the fairies became a symbol of his willingness to believe in the supernatural despite ridicule.
items.source: The Coming of the Fairies, published September 1, 1922

items.text: His execution would be helpful to German policy, by accentuating the differences between us and some of our fellow subjects in Ireland. It would be used, however unjustly, as a weapon against us in the United States and other neutral countries. On the other hand, magnanimity upon the part of the British Government would soothe the bitter feelings in Ireland.
items.context: Conan Doyle's petition for clemency for Sir Roger Casement, the Irish nationalist condemned to death for his role in the Easter Rising, signed by 39 prominent figures.
items.publicReaction: The petition failed to save Casement, who was executed on August 3, 1916. However, it demonstrated Conan Doyle's principled opposition to capital punishment and his concern for international relations.
items.source: A Petition to the Prime Minister on behalf of Roger Casement, signed by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle and others

items.text: The remarkable individuality and discriminating tact of my old master made a deep and lasting impression on me, though I had not the faintest idea that it would one day lead me to forsake medicine for story writing.
items.context: Conan Doyle reflecting on the influence of Professor Joseph Bell, his medical mentor at Edinburgh University, whose diagnostic methods inspired the character of Sherlock Holmes.
items.publicReaction: The statement became widely known in literary circles and helped establish the connection between Bell and Holmes, becoming a standard part of Sherlock Holmes creation mythology.
items.source: Memories and Adventures, autobiography published in The Strand Magazine (October 1923-July 1924) and in book form by Hodder & Stoughton Ltd., September 18, 1924

items.text: My God, if they only knew—if they could only know!
items.context: Conan Doyle's exclamation after a séance, which he credited as the inspiration for his voyage to Australia to spread spiritualism to new audiences.
items.publicReaction: The statement captured his passionate conviction about the truth of spiritualism and his missionary zeal in promoting it globally.
items.source: The Wanderings of a Spiritualist, documenting his travels and beliefs

items.text: It was a singular scene, my wife with her hand flying wildly, beating the table while she scribbled at a furious rate, I sitting by and tearing sheet after sheet from the block as it was filled up, and tossing each across to Houdini, while he sat silent, looking grimmer and paler every moment.
items.context: Conan Doyle's account of the séance conducted by his wife Jean with magician Harry Houdini, where his wife claimed to contact Houdini's deceased mother through automatic writing.
items.publicReaction: Houdini remained skeptical and the séance marked the beginning of their public feud over spiritualism. Conan Doyle's detailed account was cited by spiritualists as evidence; Houdini used it as evidence of fraud.
items.source: Correspondence and accounts documented in spiritualist publications and Houdini's writings

items.text: Convinced that the Channel Tunnel was a vital precaution...submarines could easily dive under any cordon and that it would be impossible to safely send an expeditionary force abroad without grave risk.
items.context: Conan Doyle published a warning in The Times about submarine threats and advocated for a Channel Tunnel as a strategic defense; he developed these ideas into a science fiction story.
items.publicReaction: His warnings about submarine warfare were prescient—Germany later deployed submarines to blockade Britain during WWI. The story 'Danger!' in the Strand Magazine became famous as a prophetic warning.
items.source: Letter in The Times (December 26, 1913) and 'Danger! Being the Log of Captain John Sirius' in The Strand Magazine (July 1914)

items.text: The badge of the provincial mayor
items.context: Conan Doyle's initial dismissive characterization of a knighthood when first approached about accepting one, reflecting his republican sympathies and disdain for titles.
items.publicReaction: His mother pressured him to reconsider, arguing it would insult the king to refuse. After receiving congratulations from H.G. Wells and others, he changed his mind and accepted the knighthood in October 1902.
items.source: Account in biographical sources documenting his reluctance to accept knighthood from King Edward VII

items.text: My wife and I had actually spoken face to face beyond all question or doubt with eleven friends or relatives who had passed over.
items.context: Conan Doyle testifying at a farewell luncheon in London before his spiritualist tour, claiming direct proof of communicating with the dead through séances.
items.publicReaction: Such claims were met with skepticism from the scientific establishment but belief from his spiritualist followers, deepening the division between believers and skeptics.
items.source: Testimony at farewell luncheon documented in The Wanderings of a Spiritualist

items.text: I have tried my hand at very many sports, including boxing, cricket, billiards, motoring, football, aeronautics and skiing...I have travelled as Doctor to a whaler for seven months in the Arctic and afterwards in the West Coast of Africa. I have seen something of three wars, the Soudanese, the South African and the German. My life has been dotted with adventures of all kinds. Finally I have been constrained to devote my latter years to telling the world the final result of thirty-six years' study of the occult, and in endeavouring to make it realize the overwhelming importance of the question.
items.context: Conan Doyle reflecting on his varied life experiences and explaining how they led him to consider spiritualism his most important work.
items.publicReaction: The passage demonstrated that his spiritualist advocacy was not a sudden delusion but the culmination of deliberate study. It reinforced his self-image as a serious investigator despite public ridicule.

items.context: Conan Doyle's final words, spoken to his wife Jean as he died of a heart attack at Windlesham Manor in Crowborough, Sussex.
items.publicReaction: His last words were reported in obituaries and biographies as testament to his devotion to his second wife, Jean Leckie. They became an iconic closing note to his life story.
items.source: Death account documented in multiple biographical sources and obituaries

items.text: The whole of this system...is one complete whole, each attached to the next one. We have reached a point where further proof is superfluous, and where the weight of disproof lies upon those who deny.
items.context: Conan Doyle's argument that spiritualist evidence had accumulated sufficiently to shift the burden of proof onto skeptics rather than believers.
items.publicReaction: Spiritualists cited this as vindication of their movement; skeptics dismissed it as circular reasoning. It epitomized the fundamental divide in how different groups viewed the evidence.

items.text: There has, however, been no time in the recorded history of the world when we do not find traces of preternatural interference and a tardy recognition of them from humanity. The only difference between these episodes and the modern movement is that the former might be described as a case of stray wanderers from some further sphere, while the latter bears the sign of a purposeful and organized invasion.
items.context: Conan Doyle presenting spiritualism as a historically consistent phenomenon, but with modern spiritualism representing a more organized and intentional effort by spirits to communicate with the living.
items.publicReaction: The statement positioned modern spiritualism within a broader historical framework, lending it historical legitimacy in the eyes of believers.
items.source: The History of Spiritualism, Vol. 1, introduction
